name: backup
context: Backup and restore PostgreSQL: pg_dump, pg_basebackup, point-in-time recovery with WAL archiving
info: This skill covers PostgreSQL backup and restore strategies. pg_dump creates logical backups of individual databases — supports custom (compressed binary), directory (parallel), and plain SQL formats. pg_basebackup creates physical backups of the entire cluster for streaming replication initialization or full restore. Point-in-time recovery (PITR) combines a base backup with WAL archiving to restore to any specific timestamp — essential for recovering from accidental data loss. WAL archiving continuously copies WAL segments to a safe location (local, S3, GCS). Backup strategies should combine regular pg_dump for application-level restores with pg_basebackup + WAL archiving for disaster recovery. Always test restores regularly.
body:
# PostgreSQL Backup and Restore

A robust backup strategy is essential for any PostgreSQL deployment. Combine logical backups (pg_dump) for application-level restores with physical backups (pg_basebackup + WAL archiving) for disaster recovery and point-in-time recovery.

## pg_dump

pg_dump creates a logical backup of a single database. It outputs SQL statements or a custom binary format that can be restored with pg_restore.

```bash
# Custom format (compressed, most flexible)
pg_dump -Fc -f backup.dump $DATABASE_URL

# Directory format (parallel dump/restore)
pg_dump -Fd -j 4 -f backup_dir/ $DATABASE_URL

# Plain SQL (human-readable, good for version control)
pg_dump -Fp -f backup.sql $DATABASE_URL

# Dump specific tables
pg_dump -Fc -t users -t orders -f tables.dump $DATABASE_URL

# Dump schema only (no data)
pg_dump -Fc --schema-only -f schema.dump $DATABASE_URL

# Dump data only (no DDL)
pg_dump -Fc --data-only -f data.dump $DATABASE_URL

# Dump all databases (use pg_dumpall)
pg_dumpall -f all_databases.sql
```

### Restore from pg_dump

```bash
# Restore custom/directory format
pg_restore -d mydb backup.dump

# Restore with parallel workers
pg_restore -d mydb -j 4 backup_dir/

# Restore specific tables
pg_restore -d mydb -t users backup.dump

# Restore plain SQL
psql -d mydb -f backup.sql

# Create database and restore
createdb mydb_restored
pg_restore -d mydb_restored --clean --if-exists backup.dump
```

## pg_basebackup

pg_basebackup creates a physical backup of the entire PostgreSQL cluster. It is the foundation for streaming replication initialization and PITR.

```bash
# Basic base backup
pg_basebackup -h primary_host -U replicator -D /backup/base -Fp -Xs -P

# Compressed tar format
pg_basebackup -h primary_host -U replicator -D /backup/base -Ft -z -Xs -P

# With WAL files included (self-contained backup)
pg_basebackup -h primary_host -U replicator -D /backup/base -Ft -z -Xf -P
```

Flags: `-Fp` plain format, `-Ft` tar format, `-z` gzip compress, `-Xs` stream WAL during backup, `-Xf` fetch WAL after backup, `-P` show progress.

## Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR)

PITR lets you restore to any specific timestamp by combining a base backup with WAL archives.

### 1. Configure WAL Archiving

```ini
# postgresql.conf
wal_level = replica
archive_mode = on
archive_command = 'cp %p /archive/wal/%f'
# For S3:
# archive_command = 'aws s3 cp %p s3://my-bucket/wal/%f'
```

### 2. Take a Base Backup

```bash
pg_basebackup -h localhost -U replicator -D /backup/base -Ft -z -Xs -P
```

### 3. Restore to a Point in Time

```bash
# Stop PostgreSQL
pg_ctl stop -D /var/lib/postgresql/data

# Replace data directory with base backup
rm -rf /var/lib/postgresql/data/*
tar xzf /backup/base/base.tar.gz -C /var/lib/postgresql/data/

# Create recovery configuration
cat > /var/lib/postgresql/data/postgresql.auto.conf << EOF
restore_command = 'cp /archive/wal/%f %p'
recovery_target_time = '2026-04-10 14:30:00 UTC'
recovery_target_action = 'promote'
EOF

# Create recovery signal file
touch /var/lib/postgresql/data/recovery.signal

# Start PostgreSQL — it will replay WAL up to the target time
pg_ctl start -D /var/lib/postgresql/data
```

## Scheduling

Automate backups with cron:

```bash
# /etc/cron.d/postgres-backup

# Daily pg_dump at 2 AM
0 2 * * * postgres pg_dump -Fc -f /backup/daily/mydb_$(date +\%Y\%m\%d).dump $DATABASE_URL

# Weekly base backup on Sunday at 3 AM
0 3 * * 0 postgres pg_basebackup -h localhost -U replicator -D /backup/weekly/base_$(date +\%Y\%m\%d) -Ft -z -Xs -P

# Clean up backups older than 30 days
0 4 * * * postgres find /backup/daily -name '*.dump' -mtime +30 -delete
```

## Backup Strategy Summary

| Method | Type | Granularity | Restore Speed | Use Case |
|--------|------|-------------|---------------|----------|
| pg_dump | Logical | Database/table | Medium | App-level restore, migration |
| pg_basebackup | Physical | Full cluster | Fast | Disaster recovery, replica init |
| PITR (base + WAL) | Physical | Any timestamp | Medium | Accidental data loss recovery |
| pg_dumpall | Logical | All databases | Slow | Full cluster logical backup |

sections.title: pg_dump
sections.body: Logical backup of a single database. Custom format (`-Fc`) is compressed and supports selective restore. Directory format (`-Fd`) enables parallel dump/restore with `-j`. Plain format (`-Fp`) outputs human-readable SQL. Use `pg_restore` for custom/directory formats, `psql -f` for plain SQL. Always use `--clean --if-exists` flags when restoring to drop existing objects first. See [[postgres/queries]] for verifying restored data.

sections.title: pg_basebackup
sections.body: Physical backup of the entire cluster via streaming replication protocol. Requires a replication user. Use `-Xs` to stream WAL during backup for consistency. Compressed tar format (`-Ft -z`) is most storage-efficient. The backup can initialize a standby (see [[postgres/replication#streaming-replication]]) or serve as the foundation for PITR.

sections.title: Point-in-time recovery
sections.body: PITR combines a base backup with WAL archives to restore to any timestamp. Configure `archive_mode = on` and `archive_command` to copy WAL segments to safe storage (local filesystem, S3, GCS). Restore by extracting the base backup, setting `restore_command` and `recovery_target_time`, and creating `recovery.signal`. PostgreSQL replays WAL up to the target time. Essential for recovering from accidental `DROP TABLE` or `DELETE` without `WHERE`.

sections.title: Restore procedures
sections.body: For pg_dump: `pg_restore -d dbname backup.dump` (custom/directory) or `psql -d dbname -f backup.sql` (plain). Use `-j 4` for parallel restore. For pg_basebackup: extract to data directory, start PostgreSQL. For PITR: extract base backup, configure recovery, create `recovery.signal`, start PostgreSQL. Always test restores in a staging environment before relying on backups for production recovery.

sections.title: Backup scheduling
sections.body: Use cron for automated backups. Daily pg_dump for application-level restore capability. Weekly pg_basebackup for disaster recovery. Continuous WAL archiving for PITR granularity. Rotate old backups with `find -mtime -delete`. For cloud storage, upload to S3/GCS with lifecycle policies for automatic expiration. Monitor backup success with health checks and alerting.

links.context: Replication complements backup for high availability
